findings.description: find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users', materializes every row into a User dataclass, then linearly scans in Python to find one record — a full table scan + full object hydration replacing a single indexed WHERE email=? lookup (which login() already uses).

The O(n) Python-side linear loop over all_users to match a single email compounds the full-table fetch; it should be pushed into the SQL WHERE clause so the DB does an indexed lookup instead of the app walking every row.

The 100K-iteration PBKDF2 hash (the module's single most expensive op) is conditionally skipped for locked/unknown accounts, so response time varies by ~50-100ms between paths — a measurable timing differential (security-relevant enumeration oracle; defer detail to critic-security).

_connect opens and tears down a fresh sqlite3 connection on every login and find_user_by_email call with no pooling/reuse, adding a fixed per-request file-open + cold-cache cost that compounds with the full-table scan.

self._sessions is an unbounded in-memory dict pruned only by cleanup_expired(), which nothing calls automatically; abandoned/never-re-validated sessions accumulate forever (slow memory leak), and each cleanup sweep is O(n) over all live sessions.
